section.title: I Will Extol You, My God and King
section.sectionRationale: The last psalm attributed to David and the only one titled a psalm of praise, this alphabetic acrostic, each verse beginning with the next letter of the Hebrew alphabet, gathers every theme of the Psalter, God's greatness, compassion, kingdom, and nearness, into a single sustained hymn that opens the five unbroken Hallelujah psalms closing the whole book, 146-150.
section.thesis: David extols the LORD as his God and King from A to Z of the Hebrew alphabet, declaring that His greatness is unsearchable, His compassion rests on all He has made, His kingdom is everlasting, and He is near to all who call on Him in truth, an all-encompassing praise that invites every generation, and every living thing, to bless His holy name forever.
section.frameNote: Six movements following the acrostic's flow: unending personal praise, verses 1-3; generation to generation declaring His mighty acts, verses 4-7; His gracious and compassionate character, verses 8-9; His everlasting kingdom, verses 10-13; His provision for every living thing, verses 14-16; and His nearness, justice, and the closing call for all creation to bless His name, verses 17-21.

units.label: I will exalt You, my God and King

verses.text: I will exalt You, my God and King; I will bless Your name forever and ever.
Every day I will bless You, and I will praise Your name forever and ever.
Great is the LORD and greatly to be praised; His greatness is {{cheker|unsearchable}}.

units.label: One generation will commend Your works
units.note: generational declaration of God's mighty acts

verses.text: One generation will commend Your works to the next, and will proclaim Your mighty acts,
the glorious splendor of Your majesty. And I will meditate on Your wondrous works.
They will proclaim the power of Your awesome deeds, and I will declare Your greatness.
They will extol the fame of Your abundant goodness and sing joyfully of Your righteousness.

units.label: The LORD is gracious and compassionate

verses.text: The LORD is {{channun|gracious}} and compassionate, slow to anger and abounding in loving devotion.
The LORD is good to all; His compassion rests on all He has made.

units.label: Your kingdom is an everlasting kingdom

verses.text: All You have made will give You thanks, O LORD, and Your saints will bless You.
They will tell of the glory of Your kingdom and speak of Your might,
to make known to men Your mighty acts and the glorious splendor of Your kingdom.
Your kingdom is an everlasting kingdom, and Your dominion endures through all generations. The LORD is faithful in all His words and kind in all His actions.
The LORD upholds all who fall and lifts up all who are bowed down.
The eyes of all look to You, and You give them their food in season.
You open Your hand and satisfy the desire of every living thing.

units.label: The LORD is near to all who call on Him
units.note: nearness, justice, and the call to bless His name

verses.text: The LORD is righteous in all His ways and kind in all His deeds.
The LORD is {{qarov|near}} to all who call on Him, to all who call out to Him in truth.
He fulfills the desires of those who fear Him; He hears their cry and saves them.
The LORD preserves all who love Him, but all the wicked He will destroy.
My mouth will declare the praise of the LORD; let every creature bless His holy name forever and ever.

cheker.gloss: Searching out, an investigable limit. God's greatness is described as ein cheker, without limit or searching out, meaning it exceeds every human attempt to fully comprehend or measure it.

channun.gloss: Gracious, showing unearned favor. Paired here with compassionate, slow to anger, and abounding in loving devotion, this is the classic fourfold description of God's character first revealed to Moses, Exodus 34:6.

qarov.gloss: Near, close at hand. The LORD's nearness is not automatic but is promised specifically to all who call on Him in truth, verse 18, showing that access to God's presence is real, personal, and available to anyone who seeks Him sincerely.

basic.context: David's final psalm is an alphabetic acrostic, each verse beginning with the next successive letter of the Hebrew alphabet, a comprehensive, A to Z hymn of praise gathering God's greatness, compassion, everlasting kingdom, and provision for all creation into a single sustained song.
basic.hermeneutics: The acrostic form itself teaches completeness, praise that runs the full alphabet, leaving nothing about God's character unsung. The psalm describes David's own resolve to praise every day forever; it prescribes the same all-encompassing, daily praise as the fitting response to who God has revealed Himself to be.
basic.originalLanguages: God's greatness has no cheker, no searching out or limit, verse 3. He is channun, gracious, paired with compassionate, slow to anger, and abounding in loving devotion, the classic fourfold description first revealed to Moses, Exodus 34:6. And He is qarov, near, to all who call on Him in truth, verse 18, real access promised to any sincere seeker.
basic.typology: The psalm's vision of an everlasting kingdom, verse 13, anticipates Jesus' own announcement, the kingdom of heaven is at hand, Matthew 4:17, while its picture of God opening His hand to satisfy every living thing, verse 16, anticipates Christ's miracles of feeding multitudes, Mark 6:30-44, and God's final, perfect provision in the new creation.
basic.christ: This psalm's confession that the LORD is near to all who call on Him in truth, verse 18, is fulfilled and deepened in Christ, through whom believers now have access to the Father, Ephesians 2:18, and its vision of an everlasting kingdom is realized in Jesus, the Son of David whose throne and kingdom will never end, Luke 1:32-33.
basic.god: God is exalted as unsearchably great yet personally near, gracious and compassionate to all He has made, faithful to every word and kind in every deed, the King whose everlasting kingdom sustains, provides for, and hears the cry of everyone who calls on Him in truth.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 34:6 is the source of the fourfold description echoed in verse 8, gracious, compassionate, slow to anger, abounding in loving devotion. Matthew 6:26 develops the psalm's picture of God's provision for every living thing. Romans 10:13 echoes verse 18's promise that all who call on Him will be heard.
basic.oneStory: David's alphabet of praise, gathering God's unsearchable greatness, His grace to all He has made, His everlasting kingdom, and His nearness to every sincere caller, finds its fulfillment in Christ, in whom believers have access to the Father and through whom God's kingdom, promised here to endure forever, is finally and fully established.

context.literary: An alphabetic acrostic psalm, each verse beginning with the successive letter of the Hebrew alphabet, structured in six movements moving from personal praise through generational testimony, God's gracious character, His everlasting kingdom, His provision, and His nearness to those who call on Him.
context.historical: Attributed to David and titled simply A Psalm of praise, its language reflects a king mindful of God's covenant promises, 2 Samuel 7, using the acrostic form, a common teaching device for memorization, to summarize the fullness of God's character for worshipers of every generation.
context.canonical: The last psalm attributed to David in the Psalter, it functions as a hinge, gathering the whole book's themes of praise before the five unbroken Hallelujah psalms, 146-150, that bring the entire Psalter to its close.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To praise God comprehensively and continually, using the full range of the Hebrew alphabet to leave nothing about His greatness, grace, kingdom, and provision unsung, and to call every generation and every living creature to join in that praise.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's own resolve to bless God every day forever; it prescribes the abiding pattern of daily, comprehensive praise, generational transmission of God's mighty acts, and confident trust in God's provision and nearness for every believer.

debates.question: Why is the verse for the Hebrew letter nun missing from the traditional Masoretic Text of this acrostic, and does its absence affect the psalm's completeness?

views.label: The nun verse was likely lost through a scribal error at some point in the text's transmission, and its recovery from other ancient witnesses, the Septuagint, Dead Sea Scrolls, and one Hebrew manuscript, restores the psalm's original completeness
views.case: Ancient translations and manuscripts, including material at Qumran, do preserve a nun line, most often rendered along the lines of, the LORD is faithful in all His words and kind in all His deeds, closely matching language already present at the end of verse 13 in many English translations, including the BSB.

views.label: The gap may reflect an original, deliberate poetic choice or a very early transmission variation, and the psalm's completeness is not strictly dependent on every single letter appearing, since acrostics elsewhere in Scripture show some flexibility
views.case: Not every biblical acrostic follows the alphabet with perfect rigidity, so an early, intentional gap cannot be entirely ruled out, though the manuscript evidence favoring a lost nun line is substantial.

debates.bearing: Either way, the BSB, like most modern translations, includes a nun line at the end of verse 13, supplying the traditional content and preserving the psalm's full acrostic sweep from A to Z as a picture of God's completely praiseworthy character.

study.originalLanguages: God's greatness has no cheker, no searching out or limit, verse 3. He is channun, gracious, paired with compassionate, slow to anger, and abounding in loving devotion, echoing Exodus 34:6. And He is qarov, near, to all who call on Him in truth, verse 18, real nearness promised to any sincere seeker.

typology.type: Your kingdom is an everlasting kingdom, verse 13
typology.fulfillment: Jesus' announcement that the kingdom of heaven is at hand, Matthew 4:17, and the eternal throne of the Son of David, Luke 1:32-33.

typology.type: You open Your hand and satisfy the desire of every living thing, verse 16
typology.fulfillment: Christ's miracles of feeding multitudes, Mark 6:30-44, and God's final provision in the new creation.

study.christ: This psalm's confession that the LORD is near to all who call on Him in truth, verse 18, is deepened in Christ, through whom believers now have access to the Father, Ephesians 2:18, and its vision of an everlasting kingdom is realized in Jesus, the Son of David whose throne and kingdom will never end, Luke 1:32-33.
study.god: God is unsearchably great yet personally near, gracious and compassionate to all He has made, faithful to every word and kind in every deed, the King whose everlasting kingdom sustains, provides for, and hears the cry of everyone who calls on Him in truth.

crossReferences.note: The source of the fourfold description of God's character echoed in verse 8, gracious, compassionate, slow to anger, abounding in loving devotion.
Develops this psalm's picture of God's provision for every living thing into Jesus' teaching on God's care for His people.
Echoes verse 18's promise that everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be heard and saved.

study.oneStory: David's alphabet of praise, gathering God's unsearchable greatness, His grace to all He has made, His everlasting kingdom, and His nearness to every sincere caller, finds its fulfillment in Christ, in whom believers have access to the Father and through whom God's kingdom, promised here to endure forever, is finally and fully established.

sermon.title: One Generation Shall Praise Your Works to Another
sermon.why: Piper preaches directly from Psalm 145:4, showing that it is the biblical duty of every generation to make sure the next generation hears about God's mighty acts, with exultation rather than mere information, applying this psalm's generational vision pastorally to parents and churches.

quiz.q: What literary form structures this entire psalm?

options.text: An alphabetic acrostic, with each verse beginning with the next successive letter of the Hebrew alphabet.
options.note: This acrostic structure, noted in the BSB's own footnote, pictures comprehensive, A to Z praise of God.

options.text: A strict historical narrative recounting the reigns of Israel's kings.
options.note: The psalm is a hymn of praise structured as an acrostic, not a historical narrative of kings' reigns.

options.text: A legal contract between David and the LORD.
options.note: The psalm is worship and praise, not a legal document or contract.

options.text: A riddle intended to be solved by the reader.
options.note: The acrostic form organizes praise systematically; it is not designed as a riddle to solve.

quiz.q: How does David describe God's greatness in verse 3?

options.text: As unsearchable, beyond any human ability to fully measure or comprehend.
options.note: The Hebrew phrase behind unsearchable indicates greatness with no limit that can be searched out.

options.text: As easily measured and fully understood by human wisdom.
options.note: The verse says the opposite, that God's greatness is unsearchable, exceeding human comprehension.

options.text: As limited to only a few specific attributes.
options.note: The verse describes greatness without any stated limit, not a narrow set of traits.

options.text: As diminishing with the passing of each generation.
options.note: The following verses show each generation proclaiming God's greatness anew, not describing it as fading.

quiz.q: What fourfold description of God's character appears in verse 8?

options.text: Gracious and compassionate, slow to anger and abounding in loving devotion.
options.note: This classic description echoes God's own self-revelation to Moses in Exodus 34:6.

options.text: Distant, harsh, quick to anger, and sparing in mercy.
options.note: The verse describes the opposite qualities, nearness, grace, patience, and abundant mercy.

options.text: Wealthy, powerful, feared, and unapproachable.
options.note: The description focuses on relational, merciful qualities, not wealth or unapproachable power.

options.text: Silent, indifferent, unpredictable, and cold.
options.note: The verse describes a warm, gracious, patient, and loving character, not silence or indifference.

quiz.q: According to verse 18, to whom is the LORD near?

options.text: To all who call on Him, to all who call out to Him in truth.
options.note: This promise of nearness is tied specifically to sincere, truthful calling on God's name.

options.text: Only to kings and rulers, never to ordinary people.
options.note: The verse says all who call on Him, without restriction to social or political status.

options.text: To no one, since God remains entirely distant from creation.
options.note: The verse explicitly affirms God's nearness, the opposite of total distance.

options.text: Only to those born into the nation of Israel.
options.note: The promise is extended to all who call on Him in truth, not restricted by ethnicity or birth.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's final verse call all creation to respond?

options.text: By joining David's mouth in declaring the LORD's praise, with every creature blessing His holy name forever and ever.
options.note: Verse 21 extends the call to praise beyond David personally to every creature, forever.

options.text: By remaining silent out of reverent fear.
options.note: The verse calls for vocal declaration and blessing of God's name, not silence.

options.text: By limiting praise only to the priests in the temple.
options.note: The call is universal, every creature, not restricted to a priestly class.

options.text: By ending all praise once David himself had died.
options.note: The call is for praise forever and ever, extending well beyond David's own lifetime.

questions: This psalm systematically praises God from A to Z, leaving nothing about His character unsung. What aspect of God's character have you neglected to praise Him for recently?
David commits to bless God every day, forever. What would it look like to build a daily rhythm of praise into your life this week, rather than praising God only in crisis or celebration?

The psalm calls one generation to commend God's works to the next. Who is the next generation in your life, children, younger believers, newer Christians, and what mighty acts of God could you be telling them about?
God's compassion rests on all He has made, and He is near to all who call on Him in truth. Is there an area of your life where you have been calling on God only halfheartedly rather than in truth?
The psalm's final call is for every creature to bless God's holy name forever. How does joining that ongoing, eternal chorus of praise change the way you see your own worship today?

translationNotes.note: The BSB notes that this psalm is an acrostic poem, each verse beginning with the successive letters of the Hebrew alphabet, a feature that shapes the psalm's comprehensive, all-encompassing scope of praise.

The BSB notes that its final two lines, the LORD is faithful in all His words and kind in all His actions, are supplied from one Masoretic Text manuscript, the Septuagint, the Syriac, and the Dead Sea Scrolls, restoring the traditional verse for the Hebrew letter nun, missing in most Masoretic manuscripts.
Every creature renders a Hebrew phrase literally meaning all flesh, extending the call to bless God's name to the whole created order, not merely to Israel or to humanity alone.

No em or en dashes were present in the source text for this psalm. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming John Piper's 'One Generation Shall Praise Your Works to Another' at desiringgod.org, Scripture Psalm 145:4.
